data.title: Japan's unemployment rate steady at 2.6% in November, retail sales rise
data.description:
• Japan’s unemployment rate stood at 2.6% in November, unchanged from the previous three months and in line with estimates. It remained the highest reading since July 2024.
  • Retail sales in Japan rose 1.0% Y/Y in November, slowing from a 1.7% growth in the previous month but surpassing market expectations of 0.9%.It marked the third consecutive month of increase in retail turnover.
  • The industrial production in Japan decreased 2.1% in November over the same month in the previous year, reversing a 1.6% growth in the previous month and marking the first decline in three months.

data.title: Everybody Loves Languages Corp. announces acquisition by ELL Ventures
data.description:
• Everybody Loves Languages (ELL:CA) said on Wednesday that ELLC and ELL Ventures have executed a business combination agreement  that contemplates ELLC and EV entering into a business combination that would result in all the shareholders of ELLC, other than EV, receiving cash for their ELLC shares.
  • The business combination agreement contemplates a transaction pursuant to which all the shareholders of ELLC immediately prior to the closing of the transaction, other than EV, will receive, in exchange for their shares of ELLC, cash consideration of $0.085 per share. 
  • Pursuant to the business combination agreement, ELLC and EV will amalgamate to form a new corporation. 
  • Upon the amalgamation, the shareholders of ELLC, other than EV, will receive one redeemable preferred share in the capital of Amalco, which redeemable preferred share will immediately be redeemed for $0.085 in cash.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: The Westaim Corporation announces normal course issuer bid
data.description:
• The Westaim Corporation (WED:CA) said on Wednesday that the TSX Venture Exchange has accepted a notice filed by the company of its intention to make a normal course issuer bid. 
  • In connection with the NCIB, the company entered into an automatic share purchase plan with TD Securities to allow for purchases of its common shares. 
  • In connection with the commencement of the NCIB, the company will terminate its current normal course issuer bid, which commenced on April 1, 2025. 
  • Under the terminated NCIB, as of December 16, 2025, Westaim purchased an aggregate of 336,564 shares. The company has decided to conclude the terminated NCIB early to allow for future normal course issuer bids to commence concurrently with the start of each calendar year. 
  • Westaim has entered into an ASPP with TD to allow for the purchase of shares under the NCIB at times when the company would ordinarily not be permitted to purchase shares due to regulatory restrictions or self-imposed blackout periods.

data.title: Infinitum CFO Michael Wood resigns; Ikay Deol appointed as CFO
data.description:
• Infinitum Copper (INFI:CA) announced on Wednesday the appointment of Ikavinder (Ikay) Deol as Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Secretary, effective December 23, 2025, replacing Michael Wood. 
  • Wood resigned as a director and officer of the company effective December 22, 2025.
  • Deol is a Chartered Professional Accountant who works primarily with publicly listed junior mining companies, providing comprehensive accounting, financial reporting, and regulatory compliance services under IFRS.

data.title: Bitwise MARA Option Income Strategy ETF declares monthly distribution of $1.42
data.description:
• Bitwise MARA Option Income Strategy ETF (IMRA) - $1.42.
  • Payable Dec 29, 2025; for shareholders of record Dec 25, 2025; ex-div Dec 25, 2025.

data.title: Market sentiment broadly mixed this week, shows latest AAII survey
data.description:
Market sentiment among individual investors showed mixed readings this week, according to the latest survey from the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII).

Bullish sentiment declined to 37.4% for the week ended December 24, down from 44.1% a week earlier. At the same time, bearish sentiment edged higher to 34.8%, compared with 33.2% last week.

U.S. stocks traded during a holiday-shortened week, with activity centered around Christmas Eve. Stocks were little changed during the truncated Christmas Eve trading session. Alongside index moves, several sector- and stock-specific stories stood out, with industrials, financials, aerospace and defense, and building-related companies featuring prominently through strong year-to-date performance rankings.

European markets on the other hand, were majorly closed for the Christmas holidays. Asian markets were muted amid thin trading volumes, with investor attention on Japan following a Bank of Japan interest rate increase, marking its second increase this year. In China, the central bank held key lending rates steady. The week also included economic data releases such as GDP updates from Canada, the U.K., and Spain.

According to AAII, neutral sentiment, or expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, rose to 27.8% from 22.7% a week earlier.

The survey has been conducted by the American Association of Individual Investors since 1987, in which it asks respondents for their thoughts on where the market is heading in the next six months.

data.title: Toyota sales snap 11-month growth streak as China drag deepens in November
data.description:
Toyota Motor (TM) reported a modest slowdown in November 2025, with global consolidated vehicle sales (including Daihatsu Motor and Hino Motors) declining 1.9% Y/Y to 965,919 units, marking the automaker’s first annual sales decline in 11 months.

The softer performance was largely driven by China, where sales fell 12.1% Y/Y to 154,465 vehicles. Toyota cited the discontinuation of subsidy programs across more regions, delayed customer purchases amid uncertainty around new policy measures, and model transitions, including changes to core nameplates such as the RAV4, as key factors behind the decline.

By contrast, Japan sales edged up 1.5% Y/Y to 177,130 vehicles, supported by steady domestic demand. However, sales outside Japan declined 2.6% Y/Y to 788,789 units, reflecting broader pressure across overseas markets.

Within overseas markets, North America remained relatively resilient, with sales rising about 2.7% Y/Y, supported by stable demand conditions. Europe showed softer momentum, with sales declining roughly 5.3% Y/Y, while Asia excluding Japan and China also recorded a mid-single-digit Y/Y decline, highlighting that the November slowdown was not limited to China alone, even as China remained the largest drag on overall performance.

At the brand level, Toyota and Lexus combined sales fell 2.2% Y/Y to 900,011 vehicles. 

Daihatsu Motor bucked the trend, posting an 8.3% Y/Y increase in sales to 57,271 vehicles, while Hino Motors reported a sharp 20.8% Y/Y decline to 8,637 vehicles.

On the supply side, total global production declined 3.4% Y/Y to 934,001 vehicles, also marking the first production decline of 2025. Production cuts outpaced the sales decline, signaling a more cautious approach to output as demand normalizes.

Toyota brand production, including Lexus, fell 5.5% Y/Y to 821,723 vehicles, while Daihatsu’s production surged 20.5% Y/Y to 103,250 vehicles. Hino Motors’ output dropped 24.8% Y/Y to 9,028 vehicles.

Source

data.title: Asian markets edge higher after U.S. stocks extend their record run.
data.description:
Japan’s Nikkei 225 (NKY:IND) edged up 0.1% to 50,403 on Thursday. Trading volumes were light as investors awaited cabinet approval of Japan’s FY2026 budget.

The Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP) climbed 0.56%, or 22 points, to 3,963, marking a seventh straight day of gains, the longest run since July.

Hong Kong's exchange was closed.

Wall Street’s major market averages finished higher on Wednesday in the shortened Christmas Eve session, with both the Dow and S&P 500 closing at record highs.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite ended +0.2%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 advanced +0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average closed +0.6%.

Investors will get a respite from the stock market on Christmas Day, with both equity index futures and regular trading shut. The trading of metals and energy futures on Comex and the New York Mercantile Exchange will also be closed.

Government bond and corporate debt markets will be shut as well, along with the majority of banks and credit unions. For those searching for something to trade, crypto will be available all day, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) last +0.08% at $87,716.

data.title: Nvidia licenses Groq inference technology, Groq executives join chipmaker
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA) has entered into a non-exclusive licensing agreement with Groq for its inference technology. The agreement reflects a shared focus on expanding access to high-performance, low cost inference.

As part of the agreement, Groq’s Founder Jonathan Ross, President Sunny Madra and other members of the Groq team will join Nvidia to help advance and scale the licensed technology. Groq will continue to operate as an independent company, with Simon Edwards stepping into the role of Chief Executive Officer.

GroqCloud will continue to operate without interruption.

Groq, a pioneer in AI inference, announced $750 million in new financing at a post-money valuation of $6.9 billion in September. The round was led by Disruptive, with significant participation from Blackrock, Neuberger Berman, DTCP and a large US-based West Coast mutual fund manager. The raise also included continued support from Samsung, Cisco, D1, Altimeter, 1789 Capital and Infinitum.

Groq has been targeting revenue of $500 million this year amid booming demand for AI accelerator chips used in speeding up the process for large language models to complete inference-related tasks.

Groq was founded in 2016 by a group of former engineers, including Ross. He was one of the creators of Google’s tensor processing unit, or TPU, the search giant’s custom chip that’s being used by some companies as an alternative to Nvidia’s graphics processing units.

CNBC reported that Nvidia is acquiring assets from Groq for $20 billion. Alex Davis, CEO of Disruptive, told CNBC that Nvidia is getting all of Groq’s assets, though its nascent Groq cloud business is not part of the transaction.

If the figures are correct, the deal would represent by far Nvidia’s largest acquisition ever. The chipmaker’s biggest acquisition to date occurred in 2019, when it acquired Israeli chip designer Mellanox for nearly $7 billion.
